observations.recommendation:
To enhance asset management and address the noted deficiencies, the following
measures are recommended and Management agreed to take the following
actions:

a. Conduct One-Time Cleansing of PPE Accounts: Implement COA Circular 2020-
006 to resolve concerns related to PPE accounts' accuracy, existence, and valuation.
This one-time cleansing process will involve the following:
• Conducting complete physical counts.
• Recognize PPE items found at the station.
• Disposing of non-existing/missing PPE items.

b. To ensure these issues do not reoccur, formulate and implement an Asset
Management Framework by developing and institutionalizing an Asset
Management Manual that is aligned with the aforementioned COA Circulars
and accounting policies. The manual should include:
• Roles and Responsibilities: Clearly define the functions of personnel involved
in asset tracking, reconciliation, and disposal, including the GSO Designate,
Accounting Office, and end-user offices.
• Step-by-Step Procedures/ Processes: Establish step-by-step guidelines for
asset acquisition, tagging, issuance, utilization, periodic assessment, transfer and
disposal.
• Forms/Reports: Observe proper documentation of transactions affecting PPE
through the maintenance of required forms/reports.
• Timelines: Specify deadlines for processes, if applicable, report/records
preparation/submission and reconciliations.
• Control Mechanisms:
 o In addition to the mandated annual comprehensive physical inventory of all
 assets, enforce a quarterly reconciliation process between accounting and
 property records, ensuring proper documentation of discrepancies and
 implementation of corrective actions.
 o Sanctions for Non-Compliance: Outline penalties for violations of the
 policies (e.g., delayed preparation/ updating of reports, habitual negligence on
 the performance of functions) with a graduated system like warnings or
 suspensions to ensure adherence and accountability.

c. Reassess manpower complement for the GSO Designate and match it with the
existing operational and regulatory demands and workloads. Also, implement a
capability- building program that includes regular trainings relevant to asset
management and financial reporting.

d. Ensure the conduct and completion of the annual physical count of PPE and
submission of the Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and
Equipment (RPCPPE) to the Audit Team.

To address the identified deficiencies and strengthen cash management controls,
we recommend that Management implement the following measures:

a. Adopt and/or strengthen the internal control system by documenting and
implementing specific control activities related to cash management, in
accordance with the New Government Accounting System (NGAS) Manual,
including:
 o Roles and Responsibilities: Clearly define the duties of collectors, cashier,
 liquidating officer, the treasurer, the accountant and other personnel.
 o Step-by-Step Procedures/ Processes: Provide detailed instructions for
 collections, remittances, deposits, and reconciliations.
 o Timelines: Specify deadlines for remittances, deposits, and reconciliations.
 o Control Mechanisms: Include the conduct of random audits on collections
 and deposits, surprise cash counts, evaluation of the effectiveness of the
 policies and training sessions/reorientation to personnel involved in cash
 management.
 o Sanctions for Non-Compliance: Outline penalties for violations of the
 policies (e.g., delayed remittances, unsupported disbursements) with a
 graduated system like warnings or suspensions to ensure adherence and
 accountability.

b. Direct all collecting officers to remit collections in full and prepare the RCDs
at the close of each business day to ensure complete and accurate recording
and reporting of transactions by the Accounting Office. Moreover, direct the
Municipal Treasurer to deposit intact all collections to AGDB.

c. Establish practical interim measures to ensure timely deposits despite bank
distance, such as increasing the frequency of banking trips or assigning
dedicated personnel for deposit tasks. Explore possibilities for establishing
accounts with AGDBs that might offer mobile collection services if available.

d. Ensure that reconciliations of cash balances are conducted between the
accountable officer and the accountant at least quarterly, or more frequently if
required by management.

e. Adhere to Proper Disbursement Procedures:
 • Immediately cease the practice of using collections to defray expenses.
 • Establish a Petty Cash Fund under the imprest system for handling small
 expenses, ensuring disbursements are properly documented and
 replenished via check.

To address these observations and mitigate associated risks, we recommended
and Management agreed to take the following actions:

a. Enforce strict liquidation policies:
• Require all accountable officers to settle outstanding cash advances within a
reasonable period.
• Temporarily suspend additional/new cash advances for officers and employees
with overdue balances until full liquidation is achieved.

b. Strengthen internal controls, monitoring, and accountability procedures:
• Enhance pre-approval review by having the Accounting and Treasurer’s Offices
verify specificity of cash advance purposes.
• Require notice of the status of previous cash advances from the Municipal
Accountant before approving the same.
• Ensure that no cash advances except those for travel are granted to officers or
employees who are not covered by the required fidelity bond.
• Refrain from utilizing cash advances for regular or recurring operational
expenses.
• Require that all regular expenses be processed through standard disbursement
procedures, such as issuing checks or direct payment to suppliers, to maintain
proper accountability.

c. Established petty cash fund:
• Set up a petty cash fund to cover minor and recurring expenditures, minimizing
the risk of misuse of cash advances for regular transactions.

d. Enhance training and capacity building:
• Observe/attend trainings on PD 1445 and COA Circulars, if there are any.
• Hold accountability seminars to underscore the legal and fiscal repercussions
of non-compliance.

e. Implement disciplinary measures:
• Impose sanctions, such as withholding of salary or reprimands, for delayed
liquidations.
• Incorporate compliance with cash advance policies into performance
evaluations and promotions.

To address the foregoing observations and strengthen the financial management
practices of the Municipal Government of Palimbang, we recommended and
Management agreed to take the following actions:

a. Require the requesting unit/section to ensure completeness of the supporting
documents, including the approved Allotment and Obligation Slip (ALOBS)
from the Municipal Budget Officer and Municipal Accountant before
requesting payment.

b. Instruct the Municipal Accountant to thoroughly review the completeness of
supporting documents from the requesting unit/section before certifying the
disbursement vouchers.

c. Secure the approval of the Local Chief Executive before processing payment
for the disbursement.

d. Designate authorized official to approve regularly and/or recurring
administrative expenses to facilitate and reduce the workload of the LCE such
as: payrolls for regular or permanent employees, expenses for light, water,
telephone and telegraph services, remittances to government creditor agencies
such as GSIS, BIR, PHILHEALTH, LBP, DBP, NPO, PS of the DBM and
others.

e. Submit all financial reports such as paid Disbursement Vouchers (DVs), copies
of perfected Contracts and Purchase Orders (POs), Report on Collections and
Deposits (RCD), Bank Reconciliation Statements (BRS), Bank Statements,
Report of Checks Issued (RCI), Statement of Appropriations, Allotments,
Obligations and Balances (SAAOB), and other required schedules to the audit
team within the prescribe period.

a. Direct the Municipal Accountant’s Office to strictly adhere to the NGAS
Manual for LGUs by diligently maintaining complete and up-to-date books of
original entry (journals) and final entry (ledgers) for all financial transactions.

b. Require that all financial accounts, particularly those related to assets and
liabilities, be supported by regularly updated and reconciled schedules, which
must be made available for audit purposes.

c. Implement and continuously enhance accounting systems and procedures to
support the accurate and timely collection of evidence needed for financial
reporting. This includes establishing clear internal guidelines and stricter
enforcement of deadlines for the submission of complete supporting
documents, such as disbursement vouchers, by all concerned departments.

d. Conduct training sessions for the Municipal Accountant and staff to enhance
their understanding of NGAS requirements and the proper maintenance of
accounting records. The training should focus on practical application and
best practices, ensuring the team can handle the records with competence and
diligence.

e. Establish a formal turnover process for key accounting positions to ensure
knowledge transfer and operational continuity. Provide regular training for the
Municipal Accountant and accounting staff on NGAS policies and procedures,
emphasizing hands-on application and best practices in record-keeping.

f. The Municipal Accountant's Office should assess its current staffing levels and
consider increasing personnel to effectively manage both Municipal and
Barangay accounting records. This will help to distribute the workload more
evenly and ensure all accounting records are accurately maintained.

g. To reduce processing delays, management should formally delegate authority
to a designated official for approving recurring administrative expenses (such
as payrolls, utility bills, and government remittances). This delegation must be
documented and communicated to all relevant offices to ensure smooth and
timely transaction processing.

h. Strengthen internal controls within the accounting system to ensure the
integrity and accuracy of financial records. Regular reviews and supervisory
checks should be implemented to monitor the effectiveness of the record-
keeping process and ensure timely and accurate submissions of supporting
documents.

i. Prioritize the preparation of financial statements that are accurate, verifiable,
and faithfully represent the Municipality’s financial position. Compliance with
IPSAS 1 and the IPSAS Conceptual Framework will improve the reliability,
transparency, and overall quality of financial reporting.

In light of the deficiencies identified, we recommended and Management agreed
to take the following actions:

a. Submit a complete and itemized breakdown of the ₱13,669,800.34 charged
under "Trainings and Other Expenses," along with disbursement vouchers
and their supporting documents.

b. Ensure that future DRRM-related disbursements are properly planned,
documented, and aligned with the approved LDRRMF Investment Plan
(LDRRMFIP), and that activities are directly linked to DRRM thematic
areas;

c. Strengthen internal controls by institutionalizing procedures that require prior
planning, documentation, and post-activity reporting for all DRRM-funded
programs, projects, and activities to promote transparency and accountability.

Considering the deficiencies noted, we recommended and Management agreed to
take the following actions:

a. Strictly adhere to the guidelines set forth by PCW, DILG, DBM, and NEDA
Joint Memorandum Circular 2013-01 to ensure that all identified PPAs under
the GPB directly address gender issues and contribute to gender equality and
women's empowerment.

b. GAD Focal Point System members to avail seminar on GAD and conduct
capacity-building initiatives and targeted training programs for personnel
involved in the formulation of GAD-related projects, programs, and activities
to enhance their understanding of gender analysis and ensure compliance with
GAD mandates

c. Establish a clear GAD agenda by identifying priority PPAs and targets
through consultations with local functionaries, stakeholders, and gender
experts. This should be based on the results of gender analysis and
assessment, ensuring that the concerns and needs of the LGU’s constituents and
employees are effectively addressed.

We recommended that the management further improve the level of its compliance
with the rules of RA 9184 and the quality of its procurement processes to ensure
transparency and competitiveness in government procurement through adoption of
appropriate measures that would reinforce the faithful observance of the required
activities in the conduct of competitive bidding as outlined in the Revised IRR of
RA 9184; and to promptly submit perfected contracts and other required
supporting documents within five (5) working days from execution to facilitate
timely review and evaluation.

In this regard, we recommended for Management to adopt mechanisms to ensure
adherence and compliance with the guidelines on the grant, utilization, and liquidation
of cash advances, particularly on the following:
a. Adhere strictly with the guidelines provided by various issuances to avoid
accumulation of unliquidated cash advances resulting from granting of
additional cash advances despite of existing balances.
b. Issue demand letter and require immediate liquidation of cash advances
amounting to ₱6,246,685.04 and adhere strictly with the provisions relating to
the period for a cash advance to be liquidated.
c. Allow/grant cash advances to designated disbursing officers only.
d. Refrain/stop the granting of cash advances without legally specific purpose.
e. Submit supporting documents on cash advances and check the
mechanisms/procedures on documents submission to identify existing
challenges on timely/prompt submission.

With this, we recommended for the Management to:
a. address the causes of delays in the disbursement of confidential and
intelligence fund which led to the delayed implementation of programs and
activities addressing various issues;
b. comply strictly with the provisions on the frequency of release of the fund; and
c. identify areas and causes affecting timely submission of reports and supporting
documents to improve reporting processes.

We recommended for the Municipal Accountant to implement the policies and
guidelines relating to recording and reporting PPE, semi-expendable property, and their
related accounts by:
a. Taking up the necessary accounting/adjusting entries to comply with the
₱50,000.00 capitalization threshold and recognize the change in accounting
policy, while using the new accounts prescribed in Annex B of COA Circular
No. 2024-006, as follows:
Accounts Debit Credit
Semi-expendable Property xxx
Accumulated Depreciation xxx
Property, Plant and Equipment xxx
To reclassify the carrying amount of PPE.
Accounts Debit Credit
Semi-expendable Property Expense P xxx
Semi-expendable Property P xxx
To recognize in expense the semi-expendable property acquired in CY 2023
and issued to end-users.
Accounts Debit Credit
Prior Period Adjustments / Government P xxx
Equity
Semi-expendable Property P xxx
To recognize in expense the semi-expendable property acquired in prior
years and issued to end-users.
b. Strengthening controls over PPE and semi-expendable property by
maintaining the necessary forms, registry, and reports as prescribed in COA
Circular 2022-004, dated May 31, 2022, and revisit the related issuances to
further improve recording and reporting processes.

We recommend that Management further improve, where practicable, the quality of its 20% DF initiatives delivery in order to ensure the actualization of its development goals by appropriately managing the factors affecting planning, implementation and reporting through incorporating updated data on its PPA proposals, enhancing its compliance with RA 9184, and establish necessary operating modalities that will effectively address concerns affecting the timeliness, accuracy and reliability of 20% DF reporting.

We recommend that Management ensure the quality of results delivery of its PPAs funded by fund transfers by further improving its management processes through compilation of related project documents, use of updated and reliable data, enhanced project monitoring and evaluation practices, appropriate issuance of related certificates of final acceptance, and adherence to available guidelines, laws, and regulations in the recognition and recording of events and transactions in order to reflect the correct balances of related accounts.

We recommended and Management agreed to improve its GAD gender mainstreaming through adequately capacitating its GFPS, as well as the TWG, on the technicalities and themes of project management processes; establishing and maintaining up-to-date GAD Database; and revisiting and improving management process through integrating principles of results-based management.

We recommended and Management agreed to enhance the delivery of efficient and effective COVID-19 response, rehabilitation, and recovery initiatives by improving the quality of related management processes leading to a more inclusive planning, detailed budgeting, implementation, and monitoring of results by incorporating lessons learned about COVID-19 pandemic, defining expenditures in terms of specific PPAs before funding, and using relevant and reliable data to guide decision-making.

We recommended and the Management promised to consider the following:
a. Conduct regular and periodic verification, analysis, and validation of the existence of the receivables and unliquidated cash advances;
b. Issue demand letters to concerned officials and employees with outstanding cash advances for the immediate settlement thereof; and
c. Initiate request to write-off dormant receivables and unliquidated cash advances pursuant to COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.

We recommended and the Management subscribed to establish internal controls relative to its inventories through maintenance of up-to-date SLC and stock cards, conduct of periodic physical count of inventories and reconciliation of records to safeguard the assets, to reinforce accountability and to prepare reliable reports.

We recommended and the Management agreed to enhance its current PPE internal control activities through maintenance of up-to-date PPE register/records, establishment and strengthening of appropriate property custodianship practices, and periodic conduct of physical asset verification and reconciliation to reinforce safeguard of resources, physical accountability over assets and preparation of reliable reports.

We recommended and the Management committed to require the Municipal Accountant and the General Services Officer to: (a) submit the necessary supporting schedules relative to the audit of PPE accounts; (b) conduct periodic reconciliation of their respective records to forestall possible discrepancies. Any discrepancy should be immediately investigated and properly adjusted in the books to fairly present the said account in the financial statements; and (c) to ensure the conduct of the physical inventory of all properties of the Municipal Government of Palimbang in order to ascertain the existence, validity and correctness of the reported PPE account in the books.

Therefore, we reiterated our recommendations and the Management promised to enhance the accountability over representations by improving its current reporting practices through the following:
a. Require the Municipal Treasurer’s Office and Municipal Accounting Office to collaborate in setting up a workable system of receipt and prompt releasing/submission of documents to ensure that these are submitted on a timely basis to the accounting office for recording and subsequently forwarded to COA for review;
b. Adopt appropriate measures, as far as practicable, such as hiring additional workforce or augment personnel of the Accounting Department to enhance their capability of complying with timely preparation and submission of mandatory reports, pursuant to mandated deadlines; and
c. Moving forward, strictly comply with the timelines on the recording and submission of accounts and financial reports to facilitate timely verification, validation and audit of the agency’s financial transactions.

On Unremitted and Delayed Liquidation/Deposit of Collections
1. The Audit Team recommended an overall assessment and evaluation of Municipal Treasury’s collection system against the prescribed procedures in the Local Treasury Operations Manual, existing rules and regulations and best practices in treasury operations. Also, the responsible municipal officials shall endeavor to strengthen the internal control procedures over cash and continuously monitor its operating effectiveness.

Non preparation/delayed submission of the BRS
2. We recommended that Management require the Acting Municipal Accountant to prepare the Bank Reconciliation Statements every month within ten days from receipt of the Bank Statements issued by its authorized depository bank, and submit promptly the bank reconciliation statements, including the bank statements and used checks to COA for verification and audit, in accordance with abovementioned rules and regulations.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the Management that, in order to preclude obstruction of COA’s legally mandated attest functions and imposition of legal sanctions, as well as avail of improvement opportunities that may result from audit evaluation of program/projects and operations:
1. Submit bid documents pertaining to all approved contracts within the prescribed period in compliance with the above cited COA regulations to avoid audit suspensions on payments made for infrastructure projects involved; and
2. Endeavor to accomplish the vouchers and reports properly and completely, and submit within the prescribed time lines all the mandatory reports required under existing COA rules and regulations.

observations.reason: Significant lag still exists in the submission of disbursement vouchers and documentation, reporting and submission of bid documents.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and the Management agreed that the LGUs of the province
further enhance the quality of its individual LDRRM management processes by taking
concrete actions to address the issues related to manpower, data and information, and
formalization to reinforce the establishment of an enabling level of coordination in a
collective setting and ensure disaster resiliency.

Accordingly, we recommended and the management committed to review its
current practices in relation to the accounting and reporting of DRRMF to foster
accountability over the fund as well as reliability and fair presentation of financial
statements.

Therefore, we recommended and the Management promised to consider the
following:
a. Conduct regular and periodic verification, analysis, and validation of the
existence of the receivables and unliquidated cash advances;
b. Issue demand letters to concerned officials and employees with outstanding
cash advances for the immediate settlement thereof; and
c. Initiate request to write-off dormant receivables and unliquidated cash
advances pursuant to COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.

We recommended and the Management subscribed to establish internal controls
relative to its inventories through maintenance of up-to-date SLC and stock cards,
conduct of periodic physical count of inventories and reconciliation of records to
safeguard the assets, to reinforce accountability and to prepare reliable reports.

We recommended and the Management agreed to enhance its current PPE
internal control activities through maintenance of up-to-date PPE register/records,
establishment and strengthening of appropriate property custodianship practices, and
periodic conduct of physical asset verification and reconciliation to reinforce safeguard
of resources, physical accountability over assets and preparation of reliable reports.

We recommended and the Management committed to require the Municipal
Accountant and the General Services Officer to: (a) submit the necessary supporting
schedules relative to the audit of PPE accounts; (b) conduct periodic reconciliation of
their respective records to forestall possible discrepancies. Any discrepancy should be
immediately investigated and properly adjusted in the books to fairly present the said
account in the financial statements; and (c) to ensure the conduct of the physical
inventory of all properties of the Municipal Government of Palimbang in order to
ascertain the existence, validity and correctness of the reported PPE account in the
books.

Therefore, we reiterated our recommendations and the Management promised to
enhance the accountability over representations by improving its current reporting
practices through the following:
a. Require the Municipal Treasurer’s Office and Municipal Accounting Office
to collaborate in setting up a workable system of receipt and prompt
releasing/submission of documents to ensure that these are submitted on a
timely basis to the accounting office for recording and subsequently
forwarded to COA for review;
b. Adopt appropriate measures, as far as practicable, such as hiring additional
workforce or augment personnel of the Accounting Department to enhance
their capability of complying with timely preparation and submission of
mandatory reports, pursuant to mandated deadlines; and
c. Moving forward, strictly comply with the timelines on the recording and
submission of accounts and financial reports to facilitate timely verification,
validation and audit of the agency’s financial transactions.

We recommended and the Management agreed to improve its current 20% DF
planning process by establishing mechanisms that warrants availability of relevant and
up-to-date data and information; properly functioning and strengthened structures and
arrangements; and adequacy and capacities of manpower complement to advance
coordination, cohesion, inclusion and integration in order to ensure the crafting of
inclusive, responsive and sustainable development initiatives as well as enhance its
eventual delivery.

We recommend that Management ensure, as far as practicable, an optimum level
of delivery of 20% DF initiatives through institution of appropriate arrangements and
mechanisms that will effectively manage matters affecting implementation, and enhance
aspects relating to periodic monitoring and communication.

With the foregoing, we recommended and the Management concurred to revisit
the guidelines on the provision of funds for programs, projects and activities to address
the COVID-19 situation especially on budget realignment and revision of AIP to ensure
that procedural controls are not bypassed.

We recommended and the Management agreed to establish necessary operating
modalities that will effectively address concerns affecting the timeliness, accuracy and
reliability of 20% DF reporting in order to reinforce accountability.

We recommended and Management agreed to the following:
a. Confirm the amount of payables from available records, if any and verify with the creditors to determine which of these are still valid and bona fide obligations; and
b. Make necessary adjustments supported with documented efforts and results of confirmation to revert the long outstanding obligations to the government equity, if proven to be without existing creditors to reinforce accurate presentation of liabilities in the financial statements.

The audit team recommended an overall assessment and evaluation of Municipal
Treasury’s collection system against the prescribed procedures in the Local Treasury
Operations Manual, existing rules and regulations and best practices in treasury
operations. Also, the responsible municipal officials shall endeavor to strengthen the
internal control procedures over cash and continuously monitor its operating
effectiveness.

Specifically, the team recommended and the management agreed that:
1. Collectors who are assigned in the Office of the Municipal Treasurer or within the
proximity of the said office shall remit their collections to the Liquidating Officer at
the close of the business day; in the case of collectors assigned to the field, where
travel time from their places of assignment to the Local Treasury Office is more
than one (1) day, turnover of collections shall be made at least once a week or as
soon as the collections reach P5,000.00; the Liquidating Officer remit the same to
the Municipal Treasurer for deposit to the AGDB; and properly accomplished
RCDs shall be prepared in all stages;
2. Only one stub of accountable forms shall be issued at a time to the collectors.
No subsequent issuance shall be made unless the previously issued accountable
forms have been liquidated to ensure that they are fully and properly accounted
for. Also, prepare and submit monthly the Report of Accountability for
Accountable Forms to monitor the issuance and liquidation of all accountable
forms; and
3. Immediately reconcile issued and liquidated accountable forms for each
collector by comparing the list of all issued accountable forms and all RCDs to
determine if there are accountable forms which are not yet presented for
liquidation and demand them from the collectors.

B. Non preparation/delayed submission of the BRS
Financial statements shall present fairly the financial position, financial performance,
and cash flows of an entity. Fair presentation requires the faithful representation of the
effects of transactions, other events, and conditions in accordance with the definitions and
recognition criteria for assets, liabilities, revenue, and expenses set out in IPSASs. The
application of IPSASs, with additional disclosures when necessary, is presumed to result in
financial statements that achieve a fair presentation.6 This should be applied in presenting the
Cash accounts of the municipality.
To check the accuracy and fair presentation of the Cash accounts of the municipality,
verification of the Bank Reconciliation Statements (BRS) must be done. The BRS serves as a
tool in determining the discrepancies between the accounting records and the bank records
other than those due to normal timing differences. It serves as a control mechanism to check
the accuracy and correctness of recording and posting in the books of accounts as well as in
the bank records.
The local accountant shall within ten (10) days from receipt of the Bank Statements,
reconcile the same (BS) with the General Ledger (GL). And that the duplicate and
quadruplicate copies of the BRS including the paid checks, original copies of debit/credit
memos, shall be submitted to the Auditor concerned for verification within ten (10) days for
receipt after the end of each month.7

However, verification of the office records disclosed that the accounting office failed
to prepare the monthly Bank Reconciliation Statements, thus timely verification of the
existing bank balance cannot be done.
The Acting Municipal Accountant acknowledged the above observations. She
explained that her being new to the LGU and to government transaction is still in the process
of catching up with the COA requirements. Moreover, she added that due to enormous
backlog on the transactions concerning fund transfer, she was instructed by the LCE to
monitor liquidations of the same. She further explained that upon her assumption on

We recommended that management require the Acting Municipal Accountant to
prepare the Bank Reconciliation Statements every month within ten days from receipt of the
Bank Statements issued by its authorized depository bank, and submit promptly the bank
reconciliation statements, including the bank statements and used checks to COA for
verification and audit, in accordance with abovementioned rules and regulations.

We recommended and management agreed that, in order to preclude
obstruction of COA’s legally mandated attest functions and imposition of legal
sanctions, as well as avail of improvement opportunities that may result from audit
evaluation of program/projects and operations:
1. Submit bid documents pertaining to all approved contracts within the prescribed
period in compliance with the above cited COA regulations in order to avoid audit
suspensions on payments made for infrastructure projects involved; and
2. Endeavor to accomplish the vouchers and reports properly and completely, and
submit within the prescribed time lines all the mandatory reports required under
existing COA rules and regulations.

We recommended that the management study the possibility of implementing
small infrastructure projects by administration which no contractor would undertake.
Follow through the approval of budget and establish effective linkages with the
Provincial Government office for the purpose. Consider hiring additional technical
personnel to help the Municipal Engineer’s Office in the implementation of the
infrastructure projects of the LGU.

1. Require the Municipal Accountant and the GSO/Municipal Treasurer to
maintain PPE Ledger Cards and Property Cards, respectively for each
category of asset per fund, and conduct periodic reconciliation of their
respective records to forestall possible discrepancies. Any discrepancy
should be immediately investigated and properly adjusted in the books to
fairly present the said account in the financial statements; and
2. Require the Inventory Committee to conduct physical inventory of all
assets of the Municipality pursuant to Paragraph 2, Section 124 of the
Manual on NGAS, Volume I, and to ensure the completion thereof to
determine the accuracy, reliability and integrity of the PPE account at
year-end.

2.9. We recommended the creation of an inventory committee task mainly on the
conduct of the physical count of all inventory items in order to ascertain the
existence, validity and correctness of the reported inventory in the books.
2.10 We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant and
theGSO/Municipal Treasurer prepare and maintain the Supply Ledger
Cards (SLC) and Stock Cards (SC), respectively and conduct periodic
reconciliation of their respective records to forestall possible discrepancies.
Any discrepancy should be immediately investigated and properly adjusted
in the books to fairly present the said account in the financial statements.

3.6. We recommended strict compliance with COA Circular 2012-02 dated
September 11, 2012 on the Accounting and Reporting Guidelines for the
LDRRMF of LGUs specifically on the transfer of the
unexpended/unobligated balance of the QRF and DRRMF-MOOE to the
Special Trust Fund under the account “Trust Liability-DRRM” (Code 438)
in the Trust Fund books; and make the necessary adjusting entries for the
transfer of the unexpended balance of the DRRM fund to a Trust Liability-
DRRM account.

4.6. We recommended that reconciliation between the reciprocal accounts be
regularly made for a fair presentation of the accounts in the financial
statements.

5.9. We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer
submit regularly the required financial reports, disbursement vouchers and
their supporting documents to the Office of the Auditor to facilitate timely
verification and analysis of the accounts affected/appearing in the financial
statements and attest to the reliability of management’s assertions of
existence, occurrence, validity, accuracy and completeness of the recorded
transactions.

6.8. We recommended the following:
1. Prepare the Bank Reconciliation Statements every month upon receipt of
the Bank Statements issued by its authorized depository bank and submit
promptly the bank reconciliation statements, including the bank
statements and used checks to COA for verification and audit, in
accordance with abovementioned rules and regulations;
2. Reconcile the balances per book with that of the balances per bank to
determine what caused the discrepancy. Effect the necessary adjustments
as may be required to clear the reconciling items, if any, in order to
present fairly the account balances in the financial statements; and
3. Make a representations with the depository/servicing banks for the early
release of the bank statements and other supporting documents such as
bank debit/credit memos, paid checks, etc. or request for a bank snapshot
which could be made available immediately. Also, enroll in the “We
Access program” of the bank, if any to have an updated access of the
transaction.

7.9. We recommended that Municipal Accountant:
1. issue demand letters to concerned officials and employees with
outstanding cash advances and demand for the immediate settlement
thereof, including imposing sanctions or withholding of salaries in case of
non-compliance, pursuant to the provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002;
2. request an authority for the writing-off of cash advances granted to
officers and employees who are already deceased pursuant to Section 36
of PD 1445, provided that proper documentations and justifications are
submitted; and
3. adhere strictly with COA Circular No. 97-002 and not to allow the grant
of additional cash advances, unless previous advances are fully settled or
proper accounting thereof has been made.
